does: This paper analyzes how alignment in fine-tuned large language models degrades during subsequent training, introducing a tractable alignment score with closed-form update equations during fine-tuning. The core contribution is a decomposition of alignment dynamics into two competing forces: a Rebound Force that depends on current alignment state and model distribution narrowness, and a Driving Force determined by how the training distribution relates to outcome-conditioned posteriors over aligned versus non-aligned completions. This framework unifies previously disparate explanations grounded in gradient geometry and distributional shift by connecting parameter-space learning dynamics directly to function-space alignment behavior.
connects: This work addresses a persistent problem in LLM alignment research: the fragility of alignment gains from supervised fine-tuning and RLHF when models are subsequently fine-tuned on new tasks or domains. It builds on prior work analyzing alignment degradation through gradient geometry perspectives and distributional shift arguments, while also connecting to broader literature on how neural networks preserve or lose learned properties during continued training. The decomposition into competing forces relates to catastrophic forgetting and continual learning research, as well as mechanistic interpretability work examining how alignment properties are encoded in model parameters and distributed across the network.
enables: This framework enables more principled approaches to preserving alignment during fine-tuning by identifying the specific mechanisms driving alignment loss. The closed-form understanding of Rebound and Driving Forces suggests interventions targeting either these components directly—such as constraining training distributions or modifying optimization objectives to balance alignment preservation with task learning. Future work can build methods that directly control these force terms, develop metrics for predicting alignment fragility before fine-tuning, and extend the framework to multi-task or continual learning scenarios where repeated fine-tuning is unavoidable.
